content:
8 min read
Just now

--

I read the published research on AI-generated code security instead of going off vibes. The vulnerability numbers are almost beside the point — the confidence numbers are what should worry you.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The finding that reframed how I think about this
A Stanford study (Perry, Srivastava, Kumar, and Boneh, published at ACM CCS 2023) ran a controlled experiment: one group of developers wrote code with an AI assistant, a control group didn’t. The AI-assisted group’s code was less secure. They were also more confident it was secure than the control group was about theirs.
Sit with that combination for a second, because it’s not two separate findings — it’s one mechanism. A tool that just introduced more bugs would be a manageable problem: normal code review catches it. A tool that introduces more bugs and simultaneously turns down the reviewer’s instinct to go looking for them is a different kind of problem, because the safety net you’d normally rely on has been quietly degraded by the same interaction that created the risk.
That’s the frame I want to use for the rest of this, because the raw vulnerability percentages — which are real, and which I’ll get to — only matter because almost nobody is positioned to catch them.
How often, exactly
Veracode’s GenAI Code Security Report is the most rigorous public benchmark I could find: over 100 large language models, tested across Java, Python, C#, and JavaScript on an 80-task security benchmark, first in 2025 and then again on the identical benchmark in Spring 2026. Both times: 55% of tasks produced secure code. 45% introduced a known OWASP Top 10 vulnerability.
The 45% isn’t actually the number worth dwelling on. What didn’t move alongside it is. Over the same window, syntax correctness on the same tasks — does the code actually run and do the thing asked of it — climbed from roughly 50% to roughly 95%. The models got dramatically better at one skill and didn’t improve at all on the other. Whatever training and RLHF process produces “code that works” isn’t the same process that would produce “code that’s safe,” and nothing in two years of model releases has closed that gap.
Why some bugs practically vanished and others didn’t move at all
Here’s the part that’s more interesting than a single aggregate number, and where I think the framing usually goes wrong. The 45% failure rate isn’t evenly distributed. Per Veracode’s testing across both the 2025 report and the Spring 2026 re-test: SQL injection (CWE-89) passes at 80–82%. Cryptographic handling (CWE-327) passes at around 86%. Cross-site scripting — Veracode’s own report labels it CWE-80, though the standard MITRE designation for general XSS is CWE-79 — passes at only 14–15%. Log injection (CWE-117) passes at only 12–13%.
That’s close to a full inversion. And I think the reason is structural, not random. SQL injection and cryptographic handling are what you’d call self-contained problems: the correct pattern — a parameterized query, a standard library call for hashing — looks the same no matter what surrounds it in the file. Models have seen enormous volumes of consistently-correct examples of exactly that pattern, because it doesn’t depend on context to be right.
Cross-site scripting and log injection are different in kind. Getting them right requires tracking where a piece of data ends up — is this string eventually rendered into HTML somewhere three functions away? Does this value get written into a log line that a dashboard later displays unescaped? That’s a property of the whole data flow across a request, not something visible in the local function the model is generating. It’s exactly the kind of non-local reasoning that autoregressive code generation is structurally worse at, and the benchmark numbers are a pretty direct reflection of that gap.
If your review process treats “AI wrote this” as a single risk category, you’re almost certainly over-scrutinizing code that was already fine (the model handles SQL injection about as well as it handles syntax) and under-scrutinizing the exact categories most likely to actually fail.
A three-year-old finding that keeps replicating
The Veracode numbers sit on top of a longer research history. Pearce et al. at NYU Tandon — research that later ran through both IEEE S&P and ACM’s Communications — tested GitHub Copilot against MITRE’s CWE Top 25 across 1,689 generated programs back in 2021–2022. About 40% contained a vulnerability. A separate, later study went further and pulled actual Copilot-generated code that had been merged into live, public production repositories — not synthetic benchmark prompts, shipped code that real projects were running. That analysis found 29.6% still carried a security weakness, spread across 38 distinct CWE categories. The controlled-benchmark number and the real-world number land in roughly the same range. That convergence matters: it means this isn’t an artifact of how researchers phrase test prompts, it’s a property of the actual code entering actual repositories.
The quiet one: broken access control
Injection bugs get most of the public attention because they’re easy to demonstrate — you can show a payload, watch it work, done. Broken access control is quieter and, I’d argue, more dangerous in practice, because it doesn’t fail loudly. It just silently exposes everything to whoever bothers to ask.
A Cloud Security Alliance research note (“Vibe Coding Security Debt: AI-Generated Vulnerabilities at Scale,” April 2026) puts authorization flaws and missing access controls at 45% to 70% of AI-generated code samples, depending on methodology. The mechanism, once you see it named, is obvious: ask a model to build an endpoint, and it optimizes for does this endpoint return the right data — does the query work, does the JSON look right. It does not, by default, reason about who should be allowed to call this. Authorization is a business-logic decision that has to be specified; it doesn’t fall out of “make the endpoint work” the way input sanitization sometimes does.
There’s a real, disclosed example of exactly this failure pattern, not a hypothetical: CVE-2025–48757 (CWE-863, Incorrect Authorization, CVSS 9.3 — Critical). Applications built on the Lovable AI development platform were shipping without Supabase Row-Level Security policies enabled by default. Independent researchers scanned 1,645 live, publicly deployed apps on the platform and found 303 exposed database endpoints across 170 of them — roughly one in ten of everything checked — with no authentication required to read or write tables full of PII, financial data, and developer API keys. Lovable’s official response disputes the CVE’s framing, holding that individual customers are responsible for protecting their own application’s data. That’s a fair point about where liability should sit contractually. It doesn’t change what the scan found running in production.
SSRF: the same failure, pointed outward instead of inward
Server-Side Request Forgery (CWE-918) is the mirror image of the access-control problem — same underlying gap, applied to outbound requests instead of inbound ones. Ask essentially any current model to “write a function that fetches a URL provided by the user,” and what comes back is, overwhelmingly, an unvalidated fetch: take the URL, make the request, return whatever comes back. No allowlist. No check for whether the URL resolves to an internal address or a cloud metadata endpoint rather than a normal website.
This is a training-data asymmetry, not a defect in any one vendor’s model. The defensive version of this function (DNS pre-resolution checks, IP-range blocking, an explicit egress allowlist) shows up far less often in the public code these models learned from than the naive version does. A model predicting “the most statistically likely next lines of code” reproduces the common pattern, not the rare-but-correct one, unless something in the prompt specifically asks for the safe version.
“Just have it review its own work” makes this worse, not better
The obvious mitigation is to have the model iterate — ask it to check its own output, clean it up, review for issues. A 2025 paper accepted at IEEE-ISTAS tested exactly that, and found it backfires. Researchers ran 400 code samples through 40 rounds of AI-driven “improvement” using four different prompting strategies. Critical vulnerabilities increased 37.6% after just five iterations, even in samples that started out secure. The paper’s own term for this is “feedback-loop security degradation”: without a human or an actual scanner in the loop, iterative self-refinement doesn’t converge toward safety. It drifts, and it drifts in the wrong direction just as easily as the right one.
Combine that with the Stanford confidence-paradox finding from the top of this piece, and you get a genuinely bad interaction: the tool that makes people more confident in AI-written code is also the tool most likely to be used to “double-check” that code, in a loop that measurably makes the code worse while doing nothing to correct the inflated confidence.
Nobody’s actually closing the gap
All of this would be a contained, well-understood risk if review processes had scaled alongside the volume of AI-generated code hitting production. They haven’t. Snyk’s own data — from a company that sells the exact scanning tools that would catch these issues — found that only about 1 in 10 teams currently scan most of their AI-generated code. The vulnerability rate hasn’t meaningfully declined across three years of otherwise rapid model progress. What’s actually changed is coverage, and it’s moved the wrong way: AI-written code gets less scrutiny than human-written code on average, at precisely the moment its share of total code shipped is growing fastest.
Where that leaves things
None of this is an argument against using AI to write code. I build almost everything I ship with these tools, and the productivity gain is real. It’s an argument against the specific habit of treating AI-generated output as roughly trustworthy by default — giving it a lighter review pass than human-written code because the tests pass and it looks reasonable, when the actual data says the opposite discipline is warranted.
The fix is unglamorous, which is usually a sign it’s the right one: treat AI-generated code as untrusted input to whatever SAST/SCA pipeline you already run against human-written code, not a lighter version of it. Weight that review effort against the real CWE distribution, not intuition — access control, SSRF, cross-site scripting, and log injection are where the failures concentrate; SQL injection and cryptographic handling are comparatively solved. And build in a human checkpoint that doesn’t rely on the tool grading its own homework, because the one thing the research is consistent about is that AI reviewing AI, without something external in the loop, reliably makes things worse rather than better.

Sources: Veracode, “2025 GenAI Code Security Report” + Spring 2026 update; Pearce, Ahmad, Tan, Dolan-Gavitt, Karri, “Asleep at the Keyboard? Assessing the Security of GitHub Copilot’s Code Contributions” (NYU Tandon, IEEE S&P 2022 / ACM CACM); a separate analysis of Copilot code merged into production GitHub repositories; Perry, Srivastava, Kumar, Boneh, “Do Users Write More Insecure Code with AI Assistants?” (Stanford, ACM CCS 2023); “Security Degradation in Iterative AI Code Generation” (IEEE-ISTAS 2025); Snyk, “AI Code Generation: Code Security & Quality, Benefits, Risks & Tools” (2025); Cloud Security Alliance, “Vibe Coding Security Debt: AI-Generated Vulnerabilities at Scale” (2026); NVD/Tenable/SentinelOne CVE-2025–48757 records.
